pillar.metaTitle: Adult Family Homes Near Me | Call 1-800-MEDIGAP
pillar.metaDescription: Find adult family homes near you: care levels, costs, and how to choose. Get free, no-pressure help from a senior advisor. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.quickAnswer: Adult family homes are small residential care homes, usually 2 to 6 residents in a regular house, offering meals, help with daily tasks, and 24-hour supervision. The U.S. median runs about $4,500 to $5,500 a month (Genworth, 2024). Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free local matching.
pillar.heroSubhead: Small, homelike senior care in a real neighborhood house. One free call helps you compare nearby options, care levels, and costs with no pressure.
pillar.intro: Adult family homes, also called board and care or residential care homes, offer personal care in a small house setting rather than a large facility. This guide explains what they cost, who they fit, and how to find one near you.

sections.h2: What is an adult family home?
sections.body: An adult family home is a licensed residence, typically a single-family house, where a small group of seniors (usually 2 to 6) live together and receive personal care. Staff help with bathing, dressing, medication reminders, meals, and mobility, with 24-hour supervision. Because the resident-to-caregiver ratio is small, care often feels more personal than in a large assisted living community. Names vary by state: "adult family home" is common in Washington and Oregon, while other states use "board and care home," "personal care home," or "residential care facility." All describe the same small-home care model, regulated and licensed at the state level.

sections.h2: How much do adult family homes cost near you?
sections.body: Nationally, small residential care homes run roughly $4,500 to $5,500 per month for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth Cost of Care Survey, 2024). Prices vary widely by city, room type, and the level of help required. Most homes charge a flat monthly rate that bundles room, meals, and personal care, though some add fees for memory care or two-person transfers. Always ask for an itemized written quote. We never quote a fixed carrier price by phone because rates depend on your location and needs, but an advisor can give you a realistic local range. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Adult family home vs. assisted living: which fits?
sections.body: Adult family homes suit seniors who want a quiet, intimate setting with close one-on-one attention, including many with dementia or complex daily needs. Assisted living communities are larger (often 25 to 120+ residents) with more amenities, activities, and social options, but a higher caregiver-to-resident ratio. If your loved one is overwhelmed by big buildings or needs hands-on help, a small home often works better. If they are active and social, assisted living may fit. Cost is similar at the median, so the choice usually comes down to environment and care intensity, not price alone.

sections.h2: Does Medicare or Medicaid pay for adult family homes?
sections.body: Original Medicare does not pay for room and board or long-term personal care in an adult family home, since it is considered custodial (non-medical) care. Medicare may still cover doctor visits, hospital stays, and short-term skilled care provided to a resident. Medicaid, through state Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, may cover care costs in licensed homes for those who qualify financially, though waiting lists are common (Medicaid.gov). Long-term care insurance and veterans' Aid and Attendance benefits can also help. To sort out what you qualify for, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.

sections.h2: How to find and vet an adult family home near you
sections.body: Start by confirming the home is licensed and in good standing with your state's licensing agency; ask to see recent inspection reports. Visit in person, ideally unannounced, and watch how staff interact with residents. Ask about caregiver training, staff-to-resident ratio, how medications are handled, meal flexibility, and what happens if care needs increase. Request the full fee schedule and the move-out policy in writing. Talk to current residents' families if you can. To skip the cold-calling and get matched to vetted homes near you at no cost, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What is an adult family home?
faqs.a: An adult family home is a licensed residence, usually a single-family house, where 2 to 6 seniors live together and receive meals, help with daily tasks, and 24-hour supervision. It offers a small, homelike alternative to large assisted living communities, with a low resident-to-caregiver ratio.

faqs.q: How much does an adult family home cost per month?
faqs.a: Most adult family homes in the U.S. cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Rates vary by location and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a realistic local range.

faqs.q: Is an adult family home the same as board and care?
faqs.a: Yes. Adult family home, board and care home, personal care home, and residential care home all describe the same small-home senior care model. The name simply varies by state. All are licensed residences offering personal care and supervision for a small number of residents.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for an adult family home?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare does not pay for room, board, or long-term personal care in an adult family home, because that is custodial care. Medicare still covers medical services like doctor visits and hospital stays. Medicaid HCBS waivers may help with care costs if you qualify financially.

faqs.q: How many residents live in an adult family home?
faqs.a: Adult family homes are small by design, typically housing 2 to 6 residents in a regular house. This small size allows a low caregiver-to-resident ratio and more personalized attention than larger assisted living communities, which is why many families choose them for loved ones with dementia.

faqs.q: Who is a good fit for an adult family home?
faqs.a: Adult family homes suit seniors who prefer a quiet, intimate setting and need close, hands-on help with daily activities, including many people living with dementia. Those who are highly active and social may prefer the amenities and activity options of a larger assisted living community.

faqs.q: How do I find a licensed adult family home near me?
faqs.a: Confirm licensing with your state's care licensing agency, review inspection reports, and visit in person to observe staff and residents. Ask about ratios, training, medication handling, and fees in writing. For free matching to vetted local homes, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What questions should I ask when touring an adult family home?
faqs.a: Ask about staff-to-resident ratio, caregiver training, how medications are managed, meal flexibility, what happens if care needs increase, the full fee schedule, and the move-out policy. Request everything in writing and, if possible, speak with current residents' families before deciding.

page.intro: Searching for adult family homes near you means looking for small, licensed residential care in a real neighborhood house. Here is how they work, what they cost, and how to find a good one fast.

sections.h2: How do I find adult family homes near me?
sections.body: Start with your state's care licensing agency, which lists every licensed adult family home and its inspection history. Local Area Agencies on Aging and the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov) can point you to nearby options. Then visit in person to see how staff treat residents and ask for a written fee schedule. Because there can be dozens of small homes in one city with very different care quality, many families find it faster to get matched. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, no-pressure help narrowing the list near you.

sections.h2: What do adult family homes near me cost?
sections.body: Nationally, adult family homes run about $4,500 to $5,500 per month for a shared room, with private rooms and heavier care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Your local price depends on the city, room type, and how much daily help is needed. Most homes bundle room, meals, and personal care into one monthly rate, though memory care or two-person transfers may add fees. We never quote a fixed carrier price by phone, but an advisor can give you a realistic range for your area. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What should I check before choosing one nearby?
sections.body: Confirm the home is licensed and in good standing, and ask to see recent state inspection reports. Visit unannounced if you can, and watch staff-resident interactions. Ask about the caregiver-to-resident ratio, staff training, medication handling, meal flexibility, and what happens if care needs increase. Get the full fee schedule and move-out policy in writing. If this feels overwhelming, an advisor can pre-screen vetted homes near you at no cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How do I find adult family homes near me?
faqs.a: Use your state's care licensing agency directory, the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov), and your local Area Agency on Aging, then visit homes in person. For free, no-pressure matching to vetted homes near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How much do adult family homes near me cost?
faqs.a: Most adult family homes cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Local rates vary by city and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a realistic range in your area.

faqs.q: Are adult family homes licensed?
faqs.a: Yes. Adult family homes are licensed and inspected at the state level. You can verify a home's license and view its inspection history through your state's care licensing agency before you move a loved one in. Always confirm good standing first.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover adult family homes near me?
faqs.a: Original Medicare does not pay for room, board, or long-term personal care in an adult family home. Medicaid HCBS waivers may help with care costs if you qualify financially, though waitlists are common. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your options.

faqs.q: How quickly can I move into an adult family home?
faqs.a: Availability varies by home and location; some have immediate openings while others have waitlists. Because adult family homes are small, a single opening can fill fast. Getting matched early helps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find homes with current openings near you.

page.intro: A personal care home provides help with daily living in a small residential setting, short of skilled nursing. Here is what they offer, what they cost, and how to find one near you.

sections.h2: What is a personal care home?
sections.body: A personal care home is a licensed residence that provides meals, housekeeping, medication assistance, and help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, and mobility for seniors who do not need round-the-clock skilled nursing. "Personal care home" is the common term in states like Pennsylvania and Georgia; elsewhere the same model is called an adult family home, board and care home, or residential care facility. These homes are smaller and more personal than large assisted living communities, making them a good fit for seniors who want close attention in a quieter, homelike environment.

sections.h2: What do personal care homes near me cost?
sections.body: Small residential care homes nationally run about $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Your local rate depends on the city, room type, and amount of daily help needed. Most homes charge one bundled monthly rate covering room, meals, and personal care, though some add fees for memory care or heavier assistance. We never quote a fixed carrier price by phone, but an advisor can give you a realistic local range. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do I choose a personal care home nearby?
sections.body: Confirm the home is licensed and review its state inspection reports. Visit in person, observe how staff treat residents, and ask about the caregiver ratio, training, medication handling, meals, and what happens if needs increase. Get the fee schedule and move-out policy in writing. Comparing several homes is smart but time-consuming, so an advisor can pre-screen vetted options near you at no cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is a personal care home?
faqs.a: A personal care home is a licensed residence that provides meals, medication assistance, and help with daily activities for seniors who do not need skilled nursing. It is the same small-home model called an adult family home or board and care home in other states.

faqs.q: How much do personal care homes near me cost?
faqs.a: Personal care homes typically cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Local rates vary by city and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a realistic range near you.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a personal care home and a nursing home?
faqs.a: A personal care home offers help with daily activities and supervision but not 24-hour skilled medical care. A nursing home provides ongoing skilled nursing for serious medical needs. Personal care homes suit seniors who need assistance, not constant clinical care.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for personal care homes?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare does not cover room, board, or long-term personal care in a personal care home, as it is custodial care. Medicaid waivers may help with care costs for those who qualify financially. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review what you qualify for.

faqs.q: How do I find a licensed personal care home near me?
faqs.a: Check your state's care licensing agency directory and review inspection reports, then visit in person. For free, no-pressure matching to vetted personal care homes near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.metaTitle: Board and Care vs Assisted Living | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Board and care vs assisted living: compare size, cost, and care levels to choose the right fit. Free, no-pressure help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: Board and Care vs Assisted Living
page.quickAnswer: Board and care homes house 2 to 6 seniors in a house with hands-on, personal care, while assisted living communities serve dozens of residents with more amenities and activities. Both cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month at the median (Genworth, 2024). Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.
page.heroSubhead: Small home or large community? One free call helps you compare care, cost, and environment to choose the right fit.
page.intro: Board and care and assisted living offer similar levels of help at a similar median price, but the environments differ sharply. Here is how to decide which fits your loved one.

sections.h2: Board and care vs assisted living: what is the difference?
sections.body: The biggest difference is size and atmosphere. A board and care home (also called an adult family home or residential care home) houses just 2 to 6 seniors in a regular house, with a low caregiver-to-resident ratio and a quiet, family-like feel. Assisted living communities are much larger, often 25 to 120-plus residents, with restaurant-style dining, activity calendars, fitness rooms, and more social opportunities, but a higher resident-to-staff ratio. Both provide help with daily activities, medications, and meals. The right choice depends on whether your loved one thrives with close personal attention or with an active, social community.

sections.h2: Does board and care or assisted living cost more?
sections.body: At the national median, the two are similar: roughly $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room in a board and care home, and a comparable range for assisted living, with both rising for private rooms and higher care needs (Genworth, 2024). Board and care homes sometimes cost less for heavy hands-on care because of their flat bundled rates, while assisted living may add fees per service. Prices vary widely by location. We never quote a fixed carrier price by phone, but an advisor can compare realistic local ranges for both. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Which is better for dementia care?
sections.body: Many families choose a board and care home for advanced dementia because the small, predictable setting and low caregiver ratio reduce confusion and offer close supervision. Larger assisted living communities often have dedicated memory care wings with secured spaces and trained staff, which can suit residents who still benefit from structured activities. The best fit depends on your loved one's stage, behaviors, and personality. An advisor can match you to homes and communities equipped for the specific level of memory care you need. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What is the main difference between board and care and assisted living?
faqs.a: Board and care homes house just 2 to 6 seniors in a house with close, personal attention, while assisted living communities serve dozens of residents with more amenities and activities but a higher resident-to-staff ratio. Both provide help with daily activities and medications.

faqs.q: Is board and care cheaper than assisted living?
faqs.a: At the median, both run about $4,500 to $5,500 a month (Genworth, 2024). Board and care can cost less for heavy hands-on care due to flat bundled rates, while assisted living may add per-service fees. Prices vary by location. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

faqs.q: Which is better for someone with dementia?
faqs.a: Board and care homes suit advanced dementia thanks to their small, predictable setting and close supervision. Assisted living memory care wings offer secured spaces and structured activities. The best fit depends on the person's stage and personality. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: Do both accept Medicaid?
faqs.a: Some board and care homes and assisted living communities accept Medicaid through state HCBS waivers, but many do not, and openings are limited. Coverage and availability vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find Medicaid-friendly options near you.

faqs.q: How do I decide between board and care and assisted living?
faqs.a: Weigh whether your loved one thrives with close personal attention (board and care) or an active, social community (assisted living), then compare local costs and care levels. A free advisor call can match you to the right fit. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

page.quickAnswer: A board and care home is a small licensed residence, usually 2 to 6 seniors in a house, that provides meals, help with daily activities, medication assistance, and 24-hour supervision. The U.S. median is about $4,500 to $5,500 a month (Genworth, 2024). Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.
page.heroSubhead: A plain-English guide to board and care homes. One free call helps you compare options, costs, and care levels with no pressure.
page.intro: A board and care home is a small-home senior care option that surprises many families with how personal and affordable it can be. Here is exactly what it is, what it offers, and who it fits.

sections.h2: What is a board and care home, exactly?
sections.body: A board and care home is a licensed residence, typically a single-family house, where a small group of seniors (usually 2 to 6) live together and receive meals, help with daily activities like bathing and dressing, medication assistance, and 24-hour supervision. It is regulated and inspected by the state. The same model goes by different names: adult family home, personal care home, and residential care facility all describe the same thing. Board and care homes serve seniors who need daily help but not round-the-clock skilled nursing, offering a homelike alternative to large assisted living communities.

sections.h2: What services does a board and care home provide?
sections.body: Board and care homes provide room and board plus personal care: help with bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, mobility, and transfers; medication reminders or assistance; three meals a day plus snacks; housekeeping and laundry; and 24-hour supervision. Because the homes are small, staff often know each resident's routine and preferences well. Many homes also coordinate with visiting doctors, hospice, and home-health nurses. What they do not provide is ongoing skilled medical care, which requires a nursing home. To find a home offering the specific services your loved one needs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Who is a board and care home best for?
sections.body: Board and care homes suit seniors who need hands-on daily help in a quiet, intimate setting, including many people living with dementia who do better with close supervision and fewer distractions. They are also a good fit for those who feel overwhelmed in large buildings or who want a low caregiver-to-resident ratio. Seniors who are highly active and social may prefer the amenities and activities of a larger assisted living community. An advisor can help you weigh the fit at no cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is a board and care home?
faqs.a: A board and care home is a small licensed residence, usually 2 to 6 seniors in a house, that provides meals, help with daily activities, medication assistance, and 24-hour supervision. It is the same model called an adult family home or personal care home in other states.

faqs.q: What services does a board and care home offer?
faqs.a: Board and care homes offer room and board, help with bathing, dressing, mobility and toileting, medication assistance, three daily meals, housekeeping, laundry, and 24-hour supervision. They do not provide ongoing skilled nursing, which requires a nursing home.

faqs.q: Is a board and care home the same as assisted living?
faqs.a: No. A board and care home is a small house with 2 to 6 residents and close personal care, while assisted living is a larger community with more amenities and a higher resident-to-staff ratio. Both help with daily activities but differ in size and atmosphere.

faqs.q: How much does a board and care home cost?
faqs.a: Most board and care homes cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Rates vary by location and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a realistic local range.

faqs.q: Who should consider a board and care home?
faqs.a: Board and care homes suit seniors who need hands-on daily help in a quiet, intimate setting, including many people with dementia. Those who are highly active and social may prefer a larger assisted living community. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance on the fit.

page.metaDescription: Board and care home cost explained: monthly rates, what's included, and ways to pay. Get free, no-pressure help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Board and care homes cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month at the U.S. median for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Rates bundle room, meals, and personal care and vary by location. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a realistic local range.
page.heroSubhead: Understand what board and care really costs and how to pay for it. One free call gives you a realistic local range with no pressure.
page.intro: Board and care home cost varies by location, room type, and care level, but most families pay one bundled monthly rate. Here is what is included and how to cover it.

sections.h2: How much does a board and care home cost per month?
sections.body: At the national median, board and care homes run about $4,500 to $5,500 a month for a shared room, with private rooms and seniors needing heavier care paying more (Genworth Cost of Care Survey, 2024). Prices swing widely by region, with high-cost metros and coastal states well above the median and rural areas below it. Most homes charge a flat monthly rate that bundles room, meals, and personal care, which makes budgeting simpler than the per-service fees common in assisted living. We never quote a fixed carrier price by phone, but an advisor can give you a realistic range for your area. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What is included in the cost, and what costs extra?
sections.body: The base monthly rate typically covers the room, three meals plus snacks, housekeeping, laundry, medication assistance, help with daily activities, and 24-hour supervision. Extra fees may apply for a private room, memory care, two-person transfers, incontinence care, or higher levels of hands-on help. Always ask for an itemized written quote and a clear list of what triggers a rate increase. Comparing the all-in cost across homes, not just the advertised base rate, is the only way to budget accurately. An advisor can help you compare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How can I pay for a board and care home?
sections.body: Most families pay privately from savings, Social Security, pensions, or proceeds from selling a home. Medicaid, through state Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, may cover care costs in licensed homes for those who qualify financially, though waitlists are common (Medicaid.gov). Long-term care insurance can reimburse covered costs, and veterans or surviving spouses may qualify for the VA Aid and Attendance benefit. Original Medicare does not pay for room, board, or long-term personal care. To map out which options you qualify for, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How much does a board and care home cost per month?
faqs.a: Board and care homes cost about $4,500 to $5,500 a month at the U.S. median for a shared room, with private rooms and higher care needs costing more (Genworth, 2024). Rates vary widely by location. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a realistic local range.

faqs.q: What is included in board and care home cost?
faqs.a: The base rate usually covers the room, three meals plus snacks, housekeeping, laundry, medication assistance, help with daily activities, and 24-hour supervision. Private rooms, memory care, and heavier care needs may cost extra. Ask for an itemized written quote.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for board and care home cost?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare does not pay for room, board, or long-term personal care in a board and care home, as it is custodial care. Medicaid HCBS waivers may help with care costs for those who qualify financially. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your options.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover board and care homes?
faqs.a: Medicaid may cover care costs in licensed board and care homes through state HCBS waivers for those who qualify financially, though room and board may not be covered and waitlists are common. Coverage varies by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find Medicaid-friendly homes.

faqs.q: How can I pay for a board and care home?
faqs.a: Families typically pay privately, or use Medicaid HCBS waivers, long-term care insurance, or the VA Aid and Attendance benefit if eligible. Original Medicare does not cover these costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to map out which options you qualify for.
